title: Somatic Therapy for Survivors of Gaslighting
excerpt: Discover how somatic therapy aids survivors of gaslighting in reclaiming their bodily trust and healing trauma beyond cognitive therapy.
body:
Understanding Gaslighting and Its Impact on the Body

Gaslighting is a form of psychological manipulation where a person or group causes someone to doubt their own memory, perception, or sanity. This insidious form of emotional abuse can leave survivors feeling disoriented, anxious, and disconnected from their own reality. While therapy often focuses on cognitive processing of these experiences, the impact of gaslighting runs much deeper—rooted in the body’s nervous system responses.

Research from trauma experts like Bessel van der Kolk emphasizes that "Trauma is stored in the body," which means that the effects of gaslighting are not just mental but somatic. Survivors often experience chronic stress responses, hypervigilance, and a pervasive sense of insecurity that no amount of intellectual insight alone can resolve.

The Nervous System Under Siege: What Happens When You’re Gaslit?

Gaslighting triggers a persistent threat response in the autonomic nervous system (ANS). Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory explains how the ANS regulates our sense of safety. When someone is gaslit, their nervous system often remains in a heightened state of sympathetic activation (fight or flight) or dorsal vagal shutdown (freeze, dissociation).

Deb Dana, a leading clinician in Polyvagal-informed therapy, notes that gaslighting undermines a person’s internal cues of safety, causing a dysregulated nervous system that struggles to return to baseline. This chronic dysregulation can manifest as:

  • Heightened anxiety and panic attacks
  • Feelings of dissociation or numbness
  • Difficulty trusting one’s own sensations and judgments
  • Physical symptoms like gastrointestinal distress and muscle tension

Why Traditional Talk Therapy Often Falls Short

Many survivors of gaslighting report feeling stuck even after cognitive behavioral therapy or talk therapy. This is because gaslighting fundamentally disrupts the connection between mind and body. According to Pat Ogden, founder of Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, trauma changes not just thoughts but bodily patterns and motor responses.

Without addressing these somatic imprints, therapy may fail to recalibrate the nervous system or restore the survivor’s felt sense of safety. Francine Shapiro’s work on EMDR also highlights that trauma is stored in implicit memory—non-verbal and somatic—requiring therapeutic approaches that engage more than just the conscious mind.

What Is Somatic Therapy and How Does It Help?

Somatic therapy is a body-centered approach that focuses on the sensations, movements, and rhythms of the body to heal trauma. It helps survivors reconnect with their bodily experiences, recognize and regulate their nervous system states, and restore agency over their own feelings and perceptions.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, developed methods to track and renegotiate trauma responses through gentle awareness of bodily sensations. This approach helps survivors of gaslighting:

  • Rebuild trust in their internal cues
  • Release stored tension and freeze responses
  • Develop resilience through nervous system regulation
  • Reclaim their sense of self beyond the manipulation

Key Somatic Techniques for Gaslighting Survivors

Several somatic techniques have proven effective in working with the aftermath of gaslighting. These include:

  • Tracking Sensations: Noticing subtle bodily feelings without judgment, which helps differentiate internal reality from external manipulation.
  • Grounding Exercises: Using the body to anchor attention in the present moment, calming hyperarousal and dissociation.
  • Movement and Breath Work: Engaging in gentle movement or breath regulation to discharge trapped energy and restore autonomic balance.
  • Resourcing: Building internal and external resources such as safe places or supportive relationships to counteract the isolation gaslighting causes.
  • Window of Tolerance Awareness: Learning to recognize when the nervous system is overwhelmed and practicing skills to stay within an optimal arousal zone (Dan Siegel’s concept).

Rebuilding Reality: The Role of Internal Family Systems and Mindfulness

Gaslighting can fragment the self, causing internal conflict between parts that doubt and parts that want to trust. Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems (IFS) therapy offers a framework to work with these conflicting parts compassionately.

Combining IFS with somatic awareness allows survivors to notice how different parts of the body and mind respond differently to trauma triggers, and gently integrate them. Mindfulness practices, as shown by Francine Shapiro’s EMDR research, also anchor survivors in present moment awareness, reducing the power of past manipulations.

Practical Steps to Begin Somatic Healing After Gaslighting

For those ready to start somatic healing work, here’s a simple guide to begin reclaiming your body and mind:

  • Start with Body Awareness: Spend 5 minutes daily noticing sensations in your feet, hands, or belly. Name what you feel without trying to change it.
  • Practice Grounding: Use your feet on the floor or your hands on a chair to feel connected to the present moment when memories or doubts arise.
  • Use Breath Regulation: Try slow, deep breaths—inhale for 4 seconds, hold for 2, exhale for 6—to calm your nervous system.
  • Seek a Trained Somatic Therapist: A skilled practitioner can guide you safely through the process of nervous system regulation and trauma release.
  • Build Support Networks: Connect with trusted people who validate your experience and provide safety.
"Healing from gaslighting requires reclaiming trust in your own body and nervous system — it’s not just about changing thoughts but restoring felt safety." — The Oracle Lover

The Science Behind Somatic Therapy’s Effectiveness

Studies confirm that somatic therapies engage the nervous system directly, facilitating neuroplastic changes that help regulate stress responses. Gabor Maté highlights how trauma disrupts the brain-body connection, and somatic methods restore this link, improving emotional regulation and resilience.

Neuroscientific findings from Dan Siegel demonstrate that integrating body-based awareness with mindful attention activates the prefrontal cortex, the brain’s executive center, allowing survivors to respond rather than react to triggers.

Conclusion: Moving Beyond Cognitive Doubt to Somatic Truth

Gaslighting damages more than your beliefs about reality—it fractures your relationship with your own body. Somatic therapy offers a grounded, science-backed path to healing by addressing trauma where it lives: in the nervous system and body. For survivors who have tried traditional therapy but still feel stuck, embracing somatic approaches can unlock new levels of safety, clarity, and self-trust.

The journey is not easy, but as Bessel van der Kolk reminds us, "The body keeps the score," and by listening deeply to our bodily wisdom, we reclaim our truth and power.

faqs.q: What is gaslighting and how does it affect survivors?
faqs.a: Gaslighting is a form of psychological abuse that makes victims doubt their own reality. It causes trauma that affects both the mind and body, leading to anxiety, dissociation, and chronic stress.

faqs.q: How does somatic therapy differ from traditional talk therapy for gaslighting survivors?
faqs.a: Somatic therapy focuses on the body's sensations and nervous system regulation, addressing trauma stored physically. This complements talk therapy by restoring bodily trust and nervous system balance.

faqs.q: Can somatic therapy help if I’ve tried other therapies before?
faqs.a: Yes. Many survivors feel stuck after traditional therapy because trauma is stored somatically. Somatic therapy provides tools to regulate the nervous system and reconnect with your body, often unlocking deeper healing.

faqs.q: What are some simple somatic practices I can try on my own?
faqs.a: Start with grounding exercises like feeling your feet on the floor, tracking body sensations, and practicing slow, deep breathing to calm your nervous system.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: This seminal book explains how trauma is stored in the body and offers insight into body-based healing approaches.

affiliateProducts.title: Waking the Tiger by Peter Levine
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational text on Somatic Experiencing, providing practical tools to understand and release trauma held in the body.

affiliateProducts.title: Polyvagal Exercises for Safety and Connection by Deb Dana
affiliateProducts.description: A practical guide with exercises to regulate the nervous system and build a sense of safety, ideal for gaslighting survivors.

metaTitle: Somatic Therapy for Survivors of Gaslighting
metaDescription: Learn how somatic therapy helps survivors of gaslighting heal trauma stored in the body and regain trust in their own perceptions.
author: The Oracle Lover
